info.text: CSIR NET Mathematics — Abstract Algebra: Module Theory. MCQ, 20 questions covering modules, submodules, quotient modules, free modules, projective and injective modules, exact sequences, tensor products, and structure theorem for finitely generated modules over PIDs.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is a free \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module?

feedback.text: A free \(R\)-module is one with a basis (a free generating set). \(\mathbb{Z}^n\) is a free \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module with the standard basis \(e_1,\ldots,e_n\). By contrast, \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is not free because the generator \(\bar{1}\) satisfies \(n\cdot\bar{1}=0\) (torsion). \(\mathbb{Q}\) as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module is not free since it is divisible. Free \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules are exactly the torsion-free finitely generated abelian groups.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\) is a torsion module (\(6\cdot\bar{1}=0\)), hence not free.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is divisible and torsion-free but not free (it has no \(\mathbb{Z}\)-basis).
Incorrect. This is a torsion module (every element has finite order), hence not free.

name.text: ALG34-Q02 Structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups

questiontext.text: By the structure theorem for finitely generated abelian groups (modules over \(\mathbb{Z}\)), every finitely generated abelian group \(G\) is isomorphic to:

feedback.text: The structure theorem: every finitely generated abelian group is isomorphic to a direct sum of a free part \(\mathbb{Z}^r\) (rank \(r\), the Betti number) and a torsion part \(\mathbb{Z}_{d_1}\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{Z}_{d_k}\) with invariant factors satisfying \(d_1\mid d_2\mid\cdots\mid d_k\). This invariant factor decomposition is unique. Alternatively, one can use the primary decomposition (elementary divisors). For PID modules, the analogous theorem gives the rational/Jordan canonical forms for linear maps.
Incorrect. The torsion part may be nontrivial; free modules are only the torsion-free finitely generated abelian groups.

answer.text: A direct product of cyclic groups without divisibility condition

feedback.text: Incorrect. The invariant factor form requires \(d_1\mid d_2\mid\cdots\mid d_k\) for uniqueness.
Incorrect. Simple modules have no proper nonzero submodules; most finitely generated abelian groups are not simple.

questiontext.text: Which of the following characterizes projective \(R\)-modules?

answer.text: A module \(P\) is projective iff every surjective module homomorphism \(f: M \to N\) and every map \(g: P \to N\) can be lifted to \(\tilde{g}: P \to M\) with \(f\circ\tilde{g} = g\)

feedback.text: Equivalently, \(P\) is projective iff: (1) every short exact sequence \(0\to A\to B\to P\to 0\) splits; (2) \(P\) is a direct summand of a free module. Over a PID, projective \(\Leftrightarrow\) free. Over a field, all modules are free (hence projective and injective). Free modules are always projective but not conversely (e.g., \(\mathbb{Z}/2\) over \(\mathbb{Z}/6\) is projective but not free). The Hom functor is: \(\text{Hom}(P,-)\) is exact iff \(P\) is projective.

answer.text: A module \(P\) is projective iff it is finitely generated

feedback.text: Incorrect. Finite generation is a separate property; projectivity is about lifting homomorphisms.

answer.text: A module \(P\) is projective iff it is torsion-free

feedback.text: Incorrect. Over \(\mathbb{Z}\), projective \(\Leftrightarrow\) free (for finitely generated modules), and free \(\Leftrightarrow\) torsion-free; but over general rings, torsion-free need not imply projective.

answer.text: A module \(P\) is projective iff every submodule of \(P\) is a direct summand

feedback.text: Incorrect. This characterizes semisimple modules, not projective modules.

name.text: ALG34-Q04 Injective modules and divisible groups

questiontext.text: Over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which of the following is an injective \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module?

feedback.text: Over \(\mathbb{Z}\), injective modules coincide with divisible abelian groups: an abelian group \(D\) is divisible if for every \(d\in D\) and nonzero \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\), there exists \(x\in D\) with \(nx=d\). \(\mathbb{Q}\) is divisible: given \(q\in\mathbb{Q}\) and \(n\neq 0\), take \(x=q/n\). Other examples: \(\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}\), the Prüfer groups \(\mathbb{Z}_{p^\infty}\). \(\mathbb{Z}\) is NOT divisible (2 is not divisible by 2 in terms of... actually: given \(1\in\mathbb{Z}\), is there \(x\in\mathbb{Z}\) with \(2x=1\)? No). So \(\mathbb{Z}\) is not injective.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Z}\) is not divisible: there is no \(x\in\mathbb{Z}\) with \(2x=1\).
Incorrect. Finite abelian groups are not divisible: \(n\cdot\bar{1}=\bar{0}\) means we can't "divide" by \(n\) in the appropriate sense.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) is free but not injective (not divisible).

questiontext.text: What is \(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)?

feedback.text: \(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\otimes_\mathbb{Z}\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\). Proof: \(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\otimes\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\cong \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}/(m\cdot\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\). Special cases: if \(\gcd(m,n)=1\) (coprime), then \(\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes\mathbb{Z}/n=0\). If \(m=n\), then \(\mathbb{Z}/m\otimes\mathbb{Z}/m\cong\mathbb{Z}/m\).
Incorrect. The tensor product gives \(\gcd\), not \(\text{lcm}\). The direct product (CRT) gives \(\text{lcm}\).
Incorrect. The tensor product is \(\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\), not \(\mathbb{Z}/mn\mathbb{Z}\).
Incorrect. The tensor product of torsion modules is torsion, not \(\mathbb{Z}\).

questiontext.text: A short exact sequence \(0 \to A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \to 0\) of \(R\)-modules splits if and only if:

answer.text: There exists a homomorphism \(h: C \to B\) such that \(g \circ h = \text{id}_C\), or equivalently \(B \cong A \oplus C\)

feedback.text: A short exact sequence splits if there exists a right splitting \(h:C\to B\) with \(g\circ h=\text{id}_C\) (or left splitting \(k:B\to A\) with \(k\circ f=\text{id}_A\)). Either condition implies \(B\cong A\oplus C\) (as modules). Over a field, every short exact sequence of vector spaces splits. Over a PID, a SES \(0\to A\to B\to C\to 0\) with \(C\) free or with \(A\) injective also splits. Example: \(0\to\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0\) does NOT split (since \(\mathbb{Z}/2\) is not projective as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module... wait, actually it does not split since if it did, \(\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\), which is false since the right side has torsion).
Incorrect. Exactness at \(B\) (\(\ker g=\text{im}\, f\)) holds for all short exact sequences; splitting is an additional property.
Incorrect. The sequence splits if \(C\) is projective (not if \(A\) is free).
Incorrect. Splitting requires a specific right inverse of \(g\), not just any nonzero map.

questiontext.text: Nakayama's Lemma: Let \(R\) be a commutative Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), and let \(M\) be a finitely generated \(R\)-module. If \(\mathfrak{m}M = M\), then:

feedback.text: Nakayama's Lemma (NAK): if \(R\) is local with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), \(M\) is finitely generated, and \(\mathfrak{m}M=M\), then \(M=0\). Proof sketch: by Cayley-Hamilton, there exists \(r\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}\) with \(rM=0\); since \(r\) is a unit (as \(r-1\in\mathfrak{m}\) and \(\mathfrak{m}\) consists of non-units), \(M=0\). Application: the minimum number of generators of \(M\) equals \(\dim_{R/\mathfrak{m}}(M/\mathfrak{m}M)\). A set \(\{m_1,\ldots,m_k\}\) generates \(M\) iff their images generate \(M/\mathfrak{m}M\) as a vector space.
Incorrect. The conclusion is \(M=0\), not that \(M\) is the residue field.
Incorrect. The lemma says \(M=0\) under the given hypotheses.
Incorrect. Nakayama's Lemma concludes \(M=0\), not an isomorphism with \(\mathfrak{m}\).

name.text: ALG34-Q08 Flat modules and tensor product exactness

questiontext.text: An \(R\)-module \(F\) is called flat if the functor \(F\otimes_R -\) is exact. Which of the following is NOT flat as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module?

feedback.text: \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) is not flat. Consider the injection \(0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\times 2}\mathbb{Z}\). Tensoring with \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\): \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\times 2}\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) is the zero map (since \(2\cdot\bar{x}=\bar{0}\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\)), but we need injectivity for flatness. Flat \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules are exactly torsion-free abelian groups: \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(\mathbb{Z}^n\), \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbb{Z}[1/p]\), etc.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is torsion-free, hence flat as a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module.
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Z}\) is free (hence projective, hence flat).
Incorrect. \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) is free hence flat.

questiontext.text: What is \(\text{Ext}^1_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z},\, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\)?

feedback.text: Apply the free resolution \(0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\times m}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\to 0\). Applying \(\text{Hom}(-,\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\) gives \(0\to\text{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\to\text{Hom}(\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Z}/n)\xrightarrow{m^*}\text{Hom}(\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Z}/n)\to 0\). Here \(\text{Hom}(\mathbb{Z},\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\) and \(m^*\) is multiplication by \(m\). So \(\text{Ext}^1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)=\text{coker}(m^*)=\mathbb{Z}/n/(m\cdot\mathbb{Z}/n)=\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\).
Incorrect. \(\text{Ext}^1\) gives \(\gcd\), not \(\text{lcm}\).
Incorrect. \(\text{Ext}^1=0\) would mean \(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\) is projective, but torsion modules are not projective over \(\mathbb{Z}\).
Incorrect. The formula involves \(\gcd\), not the product.

questiontext.text: A module \(M\) is semisimple if and only if:

answer.text: \(M\) is a direct sum of simple (irreducible) submodules

feedback.text: A module is semisimple iff it is a direct sum of simple submodules, iff every submodule is a direct summand. Simple modules have no proper nonzero submodules. Maschke's theorem: the group algebra \(kG\) (for finite group \(G\) over field \(k\) with \(\text{char}(k)\nmid|G|\)) is semisimple, meaning every \(kG\)-module is semisimple. By Artin-Wedderburn, semisimple rings are products of matrix rings over division rings. Every vector space (module over a field) is semisimple.
Incorrect. These conditions describe free modules over PIDs, not semisimplicity.

answer.text: Every homomorphism from \(M\) to another module is surjective

feedback.text: Incorrect. This would characterize simple modules with additional conditions, not semisimple ones.

answer.text: \(M\) has a composition series of length 1

feedback.text: Incorrect. A composition series of length 1 means \(M\) is simple; semisimple allows longer sums of simples.

questiontext.text: The Jordan-Hölder theorem for modules states that if a module \(M\) has a composition series, then:

answer.text: Any two composition series of \(M\) have the same length and the same composition factors (up to isomorphism and reordering)

feedback.text: A composition series \(0=M_0\subset M_1\subset\cdots\subset M_n=M\) has composition factors \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) which are simple. The Jordan-Hölder theorem guarantees that although the series itself is not unique, the multiset of composition factors is unique (up to isomorphism). The common length \(n\) is the composition length of \(M\). This generalizes the uniqueness of prime factorizations: for \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), the composition factors are \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) for each prime \(p\mid n\).
Incorrect. The composition series is NOT unique (different refinements exist), but the factors are unique.
Incorrect. The Jordan-Hölder theorem doesn't say submodules are simple; it's about composition factors.

answer.text: \(M\) is isomorphic to a direct sum of the composition factors

feedback.text: Incorrect. This would make \(M\) semisimple, which need not be the case.

questiontext.text: The rational canonical form of a linear map \(T: V\to V\) over a field \(F\) is determined by the structure theorem for finitely generated modules over the PID \(F[x]\). The invariant factors \(d_1 \mid d_2 \mid \cdots \mid d_k\) satisfy:

answer.text: The product \(d_1 d_2\cdots d_k\) equals the characteristic polynomial of \(T\), and the last (largest) factor \(d_k\) is the minimal polynomial of \(T\)

feedback.text: Via the structure theorem: \(V\cong F[x]/(d_1)\oplus\cdots\oplus F[x]/(d_k)\) as \(F[x]\)-modules. The characteristic polynomial is \(\chi_T=d_1\cdots d_k\) (product of invariant factors), and the minimal polynomial is \(m_T=d_k\) (the largest invariant factor). Each invariant factor \(d_i\) contributes a companion matrix block. This is the rational canonical form, which exists over any field. The Jordan form requires the characteristic polynomial to split (all eigenvalues in \(F\)).

answer.text: The largest factor \(d_k\) equals the characteristic polynomial

feedback.text: Incorrect. The minimal polynomial equals \(d_k\) (the Cayley-Hamilton theorem says the char. poly. annihilates \(T\), but equals the product of all invariant factors).

answer.text: The invariant factors are the elementary divisors

feedback.text: Incorrect. Invariant factors and elementary divisors are related but distinct; elementary divisors are prime power factors of the invariant factors.

answer.text: \(d_1 = d_2 = \cdots = d_k\) (all invariant factors are equal)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The divisibility condition is \(d_1\mid d_2\mid\cdots\mid d_k\); they need not be equal.

questiontext.text: Let \(M\) be a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module and \(S = \mathbb{Z}\setminus p\mathbb{Z}\) for a prime \(p\). The localization \(S^{-1}M = M_p\) (localization at \(p\)) has which property?

answer.text: \(M_p\) is a module over the local ring \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\) (integers localized at \(p\)), and torsion elements of \(M\) not killed by a power of \(p\) become zero in \(M_p\)

feedback.text: Localization inverts all elements of \(S=\mathbb{Z}\setminus p\mathbb{Z}\) (integers not divisible by \(p\)). In \(M_p = S^{-1}M\), an element \(m\in M\) becomes 0 in \(M_p\) iff \(sm=0\) for some \(s\in S\), i.e., iff the order of \(m\) is not a power of \(p\) (the \(q\)-torsion for \(q\neq p\) dies). Thus \(M_p\) captures only the \(p\)-primary component of \(M\). For \(M=\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\): if \(n=p^a\cdot b\) with \(\gcd(p,b)=1\), then \(M_p\cong\mathbb{Z}/p^a\mathbb{Z}\).
Incorrect. For example, \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})_p = \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) (the \(p\)-torsion survives).
Incorrect. Localization can change the module; for example, \((\mathbb{Z}/q\mathbb{Z})_p=0\) when \(q\neq p\) is prime.
Incorrect. For instance, \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})_p\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is not free as a \(\mathbb{Z}_{(p)}\)-module.

name.text: ALG34-Q14 Representation of groups as modules

questiontext.text: A representation of a finite group \(G\) over a field \(k\) is the same as:

answer.text: A finitely generated left module over the group algebra \(kG\)

feedback.text: The group algebra \(kG = \bigoplus_{g\in G} k\cdot g\) with multiplication extending the group law. A \(kG\)-module \(M\) is a \(k\)-vector space with a \(k\)-linear action of \(G\), i.e., a homomorphism \(\rho: G\to GL(M)\). This is exactly a representation. By Maschke's theorem, when \(\text{char}(k)\nmid|G|\), \(kG\) is semisimple (Artin-Wedderburn), so all representations decompose into irreducible ones. Character theory classifies representations over \(\mathbb{C}\).
Incorrect. Ring homomorphisms \(kG\to k\) are 1-dimensional representations (characters), not all representations.
Incorrect. A faithful representation would be a subgroup of \(GL_n(k)\) isomorphic to \(G\), but representations need not be faithful.

answer.text: An abelian group on which \(G\) acts

feedback.text: Incorrect. A representation requires a vector space (over a field) with a linear \(G\)-action; the field structure is essential.

questiontext.text: Let \(T\) be a linear operator on a finite-dimensional vector space \(V\) with characteristic polynomial \(\chi_T(x)\). The Cayley-Hamilton theorem states:

answer.text: \(\chi_T(T) = 0\) (every operator satisfies its own characteristic polynomial)

feedback.text: Cayley-Hamilton: if \(\chi_T(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - T)\), then \(\chi_T(T)=0\). Module-theoretic proof: \(V\) is a finitely generated \(F[x]\)-module via \(x\cdot v=T(v)\). By the structure theorem, the characteristic polynomial equals the product of invariant factors, and the minimal polynomial (the largest invariant factor) divides \(\chi_T\). Since the minimal polynomial annihilates \(T\), so does \(\chi_T\). Consequence: the minimal polynomial divides the characteristic polynomial.

answer.text: The minimal polynomial of \(T\) equals the characteristic polynomial

feedback.text: Incorrect. The minimal polynomial divides the characteristic polynomial but they can differ (e.g., for \(T=I\), min poly is \(x-1\) but char poly is \((x-1)^n\)).
Incorrect. Cayley-Hamilton doesn't say the determinant is zero; only that \(T\) satisfies \(\chi_T\).

answer.text: The eigenvalues of \(T\) are the roots of the minimal polynomial only

feedback.text: Incorrect. The eigenvalues are roots of both the characteristic and minimal polynomials (and they have the same set of roots, though with different multiplicities).

questiontext.text: Let \(M = \mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z} \oplus \mathbb{Z}\). Identify the torsion submodule \(\text{Tor}(M)\) and the torsion-free quotient.

feedback.text: The torsion submodule \(\text{Tor}(M) = \{m\in M : rm=0\text{ for some nonzero }r\in R\}\). In \(M=\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathbb{Z}\): the torsion elements are \((\bar{a},0)\) (since \(4\cdot(\bar{a},0)=0\)) and \((0,n)\) for \(n\neq 0\) are not torsion. Thus \(\text{Tor}(M)=\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\oplus\{0\}\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\). The torsion-free quotient is \(M/\text{Tor}(M)\cong\mathbb{Z}\). Over a PID, \(M\cong\text{Tor}(M)\oplus F\) where \(F\) is free (the splitting).
Incorrect. \(4\cdot\bar{1}=\bar{0}\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\), so the first component is all torsion.
Incorrect. The \(\mathbb{Z}\) summand has no torsion.
Incorrect. The full \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) component is torsion, not just its order-2 subgroup.

questiontext.text: Hilbert's Syzygy Theorem states that every finitely generated module over \(k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) (polynomial ring in \(n\) variables over a field \(k\)) has a free resolution of length at most:

feedback.text: Hilbert's Syzygy Theorem: \(\text{gl.dim}(k[x_1,\ldots,x_n])=n\), meaning every finitely generated module has a projective (equivalently, free) resolution of length \(\leq n\). The global dimension equals the Krull dimension for polynomial rings over a field. This is fundamental in homological algebra and commutative algebra. Serre's theorem: \(R\) is regular iff it has finite global dimension, and for \(R=k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\), it equals \(n\).
Incorrect. The bound is exactly \(n\), not \(2n\).
Incorrect. Hilbert proved a finite bound of \(n\) for polynomial rings over a field.
Incorrect. For \(n=1\): \(k[x]\) is a PID, and over a PID all modules have resolutions of length \(\leq 1\) (this is the case \(n=1\)). For \(n\geq 2\), the bound is \(n\).

questiontext.text: The annihilator of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module \(M = \mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\) is the ideal \(\text{Ann}(M) = \{r\in\mathbb{Z}: rM=0\}\). What is \(\text{Ann}(M)\)?

feedback.text: \(\text{Ann}(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}) = \{r\in\mathbb{Z}: r\cdot\bar{x}=\bar{0}\ \forall\bar{x}\in\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\}\). Since \(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}=\langle\bar{1}\rangle\), we need \(r\cdot\bar{1}=\bar{0}\), i.e., \(6\mid r\). So \(\text{Ann}(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z})=6\mathbb{Z}\). In general, \(\text{Ann}(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})=n\mathbb{Z}\). The annihilator ideal determines the ring over which the module is naturally a module: \(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\) is naturally a \(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\)-module.
Incorrect. \(6\cdot\bar{x}=\bar{0}\) for all \(\bar{x}\in\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}\), so 6 is in the annihilator.
Incorrect. \(2\cdot\bar{3}=\bar{6}=\bar{0}\) but \(2\cdot\bar{1}=\bar{2}\neq\bar{0}\), so 2 is not in \(\text{Ann}(M)\).
Incorrect. \(1\cdot\bar{1}=\bar{1}\neq\bar{0}\), so 1 is not in the annihilator.

name.text: ALG34-Q19 Module homomorphism and Hom functor

questiontext.text: For \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules, what is \(\text{Hom}_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z},\, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\)?

feedback.text: A homomorphism \(f:\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) is determined by \(f(\bar{1})\). We need \(m\cdot f(\bar{1})=\bar{0}\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), i.e., \(n\mid m\cdot f(\bar{1})\). Writing \(d=\gcd(m,n)\), the possible values of \(f(\bar{1})\) are multiples of \(n/d\) in \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\). There are \(d = \gcd(m,n)\) such values, and \(\text{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\). Compare with \(\text{Ext}^1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)\mathbb{Z}\) as well.
Incorrect. The formula gives \(\gcd\), not \(\text{lcm}\).
Incorrect. When \(\gcd(m,n)>1\), there are nontrivial homomorphisms.
Incorrect. The Hom set has \(\gcd(m,n)\) elements, not \(mn\).

questiontext.text: The \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module \(M = \mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\) has a primary decomposition. Which is correct?

feedback.text: By the primary decomposition (elementary divisors): \(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/p_1^{a_1}\mathbb{Z}\oplus\cdots\) where \(n=\prod p_i^{a_i}\). Since \(12=2^2\cdot 3\), we get \(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\). This follows from CRT since \(\gcd(4,3)=1\). The 2-primary component is \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) and the 3-primary component is \(\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\). Note the invariant factor form is just \(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}\) (single invariant factor 12).
Incorrect. \(|\mathbb{Z}/6\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2|=12\) but the elementary divisors must be prime powers; 6 is not a prime power.

Incorrect. \(|\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3|=12\) but the 2-primary part should be \(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}\) (order 4), not \(\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\) (since \(12=4\times 3\), and \(4=2^2\) gives a single \(\mathbb{Z}/4\) factor).
Incorrect. Adding a \(\mathbb{Z}\) summand would give an infinite group.
